meta.title: 2.3 Load Balancing
meta.description: L4 vs L7 load balancing, algorithms (round robin, least connections, consistent hashing), health checks.

blocks.prompt: You have 5 web servers behind a load balancer. One server is 2x faster than the others. How should the load balancer distribute traffic? What if one server starts failing — how should the load balancer respond?

blocks.content:
## What load balancers do

A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed, improve availability, and enable horizontal scaling.

### L4 vs L7 load balancing

**Layer 4 (Transport):** Makes routing decisions based on IP address and TCP/UDP port. Does not inspect the request content. Fast because it operates at the network level.

**Layer 7 (Application):** Inspects HTTP headers, URLs, cookies, and request bodies. Can make intelligent routing decisions based on content.

| Feature | L4 | L7 |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (no content inspection) | Slower (parses HTTP) |
| Routing granularity | IP + port only | URL path, headers, cookies |
| SSL termination | Usually pass-through | Terminates SSL, inspects content |
| Use case | High-throughput TCP traffic | HTTP APIs, microservices |
| Example | AWS NLB, HAProxy (TCP mode) | AWS ALB, Nginx, HAProxy (HTTP mode) |

**L7 enables:** Path-based routing (`/api/*` → API servers, `/static/*` → CDN origin), header-based routing (mobile vs desktop), A/B testing, canary deployments.

### Load balancing algorithms

**Round Robin:** Requests go to servers in order: 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3... Simple but ignores server capacity and current load.

**Weighted Round Robin:** Server 1 gets 3x traffic, Server 2 gets 1x. Useful when servers have different capacities.

**Least Connections:** Send to the server with the fewest active connections. Adapts to server speed — fast servers finish requests and become available sooner.

**Least Response Time:** Send to the server with the fastest recent response time. Best for heterogeneous server pools.

**IP Hash:** Hash the client IP to determine the server. Same client always goes to the same server (sticky sessions without cookies).

**Consistent Hashing:** Advanced form of hashing that minimizes redistribution when servers are added/removed. Used by Cassandra, DynamoDB, and many caching layers.

### Health checks

Load balancers must detect failed servers and stop sending traffic to them.

**Passive health checks:** Monitor responses from normal traffic. If a server returns 5xx errors or times out, mark it unhealthy.

**Active health checks:** Periodically send probe requests to a health endpoint (e.g., `GET /health`). If the server fails N consecutive checks, remove it from the pool.

**Graceful degradation:** When a server is marked unhealthy, existing connections may be allowed to drain (complete) while new connections go elsewhere.

problems.question: A microservices architecture has a user-facing API gateway that needs to route requests to different backend services based on the URL path (/users/*, /orders/*, /search/*). Which load balancing approach is required?
options: A) L4 load balancing with IP hash
B) L7 load balancing with path-based routing
problems.explanation: Path-based routing requires inspecting the HTTP request URL, which is only possible at Layer 7. L4 (option A) cannot inspect HTTP content — it only sees IP addresses and ports. DNS round robin (option C) distributes traffic across servers but cannot route different paths to different services. This is a textbook L7 load balancing use case and exactly what AWS ALB, Nginx, and Envoy are designed for.

problems.question: Your load balancer uses round-robin across 4 servers. Server 3 has a memory leak that causes it to slow down over time, taking 5x longer to process requests. What happens to the system, and which algorithm would handle this better?
options: A) The system remains healthy because round-robin evenly distributes load; no algorithm change needed
B) Server 3 accumulates a backlog of slow requests, eventually causing timeouts; least-connections would adapt by sending fewer requests to Server 3
C) The load balancer automatically detects the slowdown and removes Server 3 from the pool
problems.explanation: Round robin blindly sends every 4th request to Server 3 regardless of its performance. Since Server 3 takes 5x longer, it accumulates connections while the other servers finish quickly. This creates a growing backlog on Server 3, eventually causing timeouts and errors for 25% of users. Least-connections would naturally adapt: since Server 3 finishes requests slowly, it always has more active connections, so the algorithm sends fewer new requests to it. Option A is wrong (the system degrades). Option C describes health checks, not the load balancing algorithm — and health checks typically look for failures, not slowdowns.

blocks.content: L4 load balancers are fast but blind to content; L7 load balancers can route based on URLs, headers, and cookies. Least-connections adapts to heterogeneous server performance better than round-robin. Health checks (active and passive) are essential for removing failed servers from the pool.

blocks.prompt: Load balancers distribute traffic. Module 2.4 covers reverse proxies and API gateways — the layer that adds authentication, rate limiting, request transformation, and other cross-cutting concerns before traffic reaches your services.
